title: Verb ないものだろうか
titleEn: Expressing hope or wonder if something can be done: 'Isn't there a way...?', 'Can't we...?'
structure: Verb-negative (often negative potential) + ものだろうか
structureEn: Verb-negative (often negative potential) + ものだろうか
explanation: Expressing hope or wonder if something can be done: 'Isn't there a way...?', 'Can't we...?' (Ngu?n: Hanabira; b?n d?ch Vi?t c?n bi?n t?p.)
explanationEn: This grammar point is used when the speaker expresses the hope, wish, or wonder if an action could be possible. It creates the nuance of 'Isn't there some way...?' or 'Can't I/you/we...?' in English, often with the negative potential form.

title: Verb ないものでもない
titleEn: Expresses the idea that it's not entirely impossible or non-existent: 'It's not that I don't...', 'It's not that there aren't...'
structure: Verb-negative form (ない form) + ものでもない
structureEn: Verb-negative form (ない form) + ものでもない
explanation: Expresses the idea that it's not entirely impossible or non-existent: 'It's not that I don't...', 'It's not that there aren't...' (Ngu?n: Hanabira; b?n d?ch Vi?t c?n bi?n t?p.)
explanationEn: The grammar point Verbないものでもない is used to express that a situation is not completely impossible or nonexistent. Literally, it can be translated as 'it's not that there aren't...' or 'it's not that I never...'. It implies that some possibility or partial truth exists.

title: Verb の ない Noun
titleEn: Used to modify a noun with a negative verb form; 'a Noun without (Verb)'.
structure: Verb in negative form + の + Noun
structureEn: Verb in negative form + の + Noun
explanation: Used to modify a noun with a negative verb form; 'a Noun without (Verb)'. (Ngu?n: Hanabira; b?n d?ch Vi?t c?n bi?n t?p.)
explanationEn: VerbのないNoun modifies a noun by describing the absence of the verb's action. It indicates 'a Noun where the action does not happen' or 'a Noun without that action.'

title: Verb ば きりがない
titleEn: Indicates there's no end once you start listing, doing, or saying something: 'If one starts to..., there will be no end.'
structure: Verb-conditional form (ば) + きりがない
structureEn: Verb-conditional form (ば) + きりがない
explanation: Indicates there's no end once you start listing, doing, or saying something: 'If one starts to..., there will be no end.' (Ngu?n: Hanabira; b?n d?ch Vi?t c?n bi?n t?p.)
explanationEn: The pattern ～ばきりがない is used when expressing that if you start an action—like listing examples or complaining—there is no end. It literally means 'if (you) do X, there's no limit/end.'

title: Verb もしないで
explanation: Means 'without even doing'. (Ngu?n: Hanabira; b?n d?ch Vi?t c?n bi?n t?p.)
explanationEn: This pattern is used to express that one has not done (or not even tried to do) some action before proceeding to do something else. It emphasizes doing something 'without even' doing the prior action.

title: Verb やしない
titleEn: Expresses a strong denial or refusal: 'I would never...', 'No way I would...'.
explanation: Expresses a strong denial or refusal: 'I would never...', 'No way I would...'. (Ngu?n: Hanabira; b?n d?ch Vi?t c?n bi?n t?p.)
explanationEn: This grammar adds a nuance of strong denial or dismissal of the idea that the speaker (or someone) would do the action. Formed by adding やしない to the verb's stem, it often appears in casual or spoken language.

title: Verb よう
titleEn: Expresses 'the way/manner of doing something' or 'in order to / so that / try to'.
structure: Verb (dictionary or negative form) + ように (する / etc.)
structureEn: Verb (dictionary or negative form) + ように (する / etc.)
explanation: Expresses 'the way/manner of doing something' or 'in order to / so that / try to'. (Ngu?n: Hanabira; b?n d?ch Vi?t c?n bi?n t?p.)
explanationEn: This usage of よう most commonly appears as 「Verb辞書形 + ように」. It can mean 'in order to do something', 'so that something happens', or 'in the way someone does something'. It is also used in expressions like ～ようにする (to make an effort to) or ～ように言う (to tell someone to do something).

title: Verb ようか Verbるまいか
titleEn: Expresses indecision: 'Should I do it or not?'.
structure: Verb-volitional form + か + Verb-volitional negative form (まい) + か
structureEn: Verb-volitional form + か + Verb-volitional negative form (まい) + か
explanation: Expresses indecision: 'Should I do it or not?'. (Ngu?n: Hanabira; b?n d?ch Vi?t c?n bi?n t?p.)
explanationEn: This grammar shows hesitation about whether to do something or not. The volitional form plus まい (negative volitional) suggests an internal debate about two opposite choices of the *same* verb.

title: Verb ようが Verb るまいが
titleEn: Expresses 'whether or not', 'no matter if something happens or not'.
structure: Verb-volitional form + が + Verb-negative volitional form + が
structureEn: Verb-volitional form + が + Verb-negative volitional form + が
explanation: Expresses 'whether or not', 'no matter if something happens or not'. (Ngu?n: Hanabira; b?n d?ch Vi?t c?n bi?n t?p.)
explanationEn: Verbようが Verbるまいが indicates that whatever happens—whether the action is done or not—the outcome remains unchanged or the speaker's stance remains the same.

title: Verb ようがない
titleEn: Expresses 'no way to do something,' 'impossible to do'.
explanation: Expresses 'no way to do something,' 'impossible to do'. (Ngu?n: Hanabira; b?n d?ch Vi?t c?n bi?n t?p.)
explanationEn: 〜ようがない is used to say that there's no way or method to do something, even if you wanted to.
